items.mistake: Adding the gradient instead of subtracting when minimizing loss
items.fix: Use θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) for minimization; add η∇L only when intentionally maximizing.
items.why: ∇L points toward steepest loss increase; adding it climbs the surface locally.
items.example: With ∇L = 3 and η = 0.1, correct descent gives θ − 0.3; adding yields θ + 0.3 and loss rises to first order.

items.mistake: Calling mini-batch training batch gradient descent
items.fix: Reserve batch GD for updates where ∇L averages over the entire training set; mini-batch methods are SGD variants.
items.why: Terminology drift hides whether gradients are exact or noisy, affecting expectations about convergence and cost.
items.example: Updating every 32 samples is mini-batch SGD with batch size 32, not full-batch GD—even if people colloquially say batch size.

items.mistake: Forgetting to zero gradients between epochs
items.fix: Call `optimizer.zero_grad()` before each `backward()` unless intentional gradient accumulation is configured.
items.why: Frameworks accumulate gradients by default; stale values corrupt the θ ← θ − η∇L update.
items.example: Two backward passes without zeroing doubles every `.grad` entry, effectively using η′ = 2η.

items.mistake: Expecting one epoch of batch GD to fully train a deep network
items.fix: Plan for many epochs or switch to mini-batch SGD; one gradient step is only a local linear move on a complex surface.
items.why: A single update rarely reaches a good minimum; non-convexity and scale require iterative refinement.
items.example: One full-batch step on ImageNet-scale data changes weights infinitesimally relative to the full training trajectory.
